events.text: Patient is a 72-year-old Black male with a 60 pack-year smoking history (current smoker), ECOG performance status 1, BMI 22.8.

At age 72, patient was diagnosed with non-small cell lung cancer, squamous cell carcinoma (histology 8070), primary site left upper lobe (C34.1), clinical stage IIIB, T4N2M0, with chest wall invasion.
At age 72, patient presented with left-sided chest pain, progressive dyspnea on exertion, and a 20-pound weight loss over 3 months. Heavy smoking history with known COPD.

At age 72, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast demonstrated a 6.1 x 5.3 cm mass in the left upper lobe with invasion into the chest wall and destruction of the adjacent 3rd rib. Ipsilateral mediastinal and subcarinal lymphadenopathy measuring up to 2.8 cm in short axis. No distant metastases identified.

At age 72, PET/CT showed hypermetabolic left upper lobe mass (SUVmax 18.7) with chest wall invasion, hypermetabolic left hilar, aortopulmonary window, and subcarinal lymph nodes (SUVmax 9.4). No distant metastatic disease.
At age 72, MRI brain with and without contrast showed no intracranial metastatic disease.

At age 72, bronchoscopic biopsy of left upper lobe endobronchial lesion revealed poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma. Immunohistochemistry: p40 positive, CK5/6 positive, TTF-1 negative, Napsin-A negative. PD-L1 TPS 30% (22C3 assay).

At age 72, next-generation sequencing (Tempus xT) of tumor specimen: EGFR wild-type, ALK rearrangement not detected, ROS1 rearrangement not detected, BRAF mutation not detected, PIK3CA E545K detected, TP53 R248W detected, CDKN2A loss detected. No actionable driver mutations identified. Tumor mutational burden 14.8 mut/Mb. Microsatellite stable.

At age 72, multidisciplinary tumor board discussion: unresectable stage IIIB squamous cell carcinoma. Recommended concurrent chemoradiation with carboplatin/paclitaxel followed by durvalumab consolidation, but given borderline pulmonary function (FEV1 1.4L, 48% predicted), concern about radiation pneumonitis. Decision made for systemic chemotherapy with carboplatin/paclitaxel/pembrolizumab.
At age 72, initiated carboplatin AUC 6, paclitaxel 200 mg/m2, and pembrolizumab 200 mg IV every 3 weeks for unresectable stage IIIB squamous NSCLC.
At age 72, after cycle 1, patient developed grade 2 nausea managed with ondansetron and dexamethasone. Grade 1 fatigue reported.
At age 72, after cycle 2, patient reported grade 1 peripheral neuropathy in bilateral feet with tingling and numbness.
At age 72, restaging CT after 4 cycles of carboplatin/paclitaxel/pembrolizumab showed partial response with primary mass decreased to 4.0 x 3.2 cm. Lymphadenopathy decreased. No new lesions.
At age 72, completed 4 cycles of carboplatin/paclitaxel/pembrolizumab. Transitioned to pembrolizumab 200 mg IV every 3 weeks maintenance.

At age 73, after cycle 6 of pembrolizumab maintenance, patient developed grade 2 peripheral neuropathy with difficulty with fine motor tasks. Paclitaxel previously discontinued; neuropathy persisting.
At age 73, restaging CT after 6 cycles of pembrolizumab maintenance showed stable partial response. Primary mass 3.8 x 3.0 cm.

At age 73, after cycle 10 of pembrolizumab maintenance, patient developed new dry cough and dyspnea. CT chest showed ground-glass opacities in bilateral lower lobes suspicious for immune-related pneumonitis.

At age 73, pembrolizumab held due to suspected grade 2 immune-related pneumonitis. Started prednisone 1 mg/kg with plan for slow taper over 6 weeks. Pulmonary function tests showed new restrictive pattern.
At age 73, grade 2 immune-related pneumonitis confirmed. Symptoms improved after 2 weeks of corticosteroids. Prednisone taper initiated.

At age 73, pneumonitis resolved after 6-week steroid taper. Given grade 2 pneumonitis, decision to rechallenge pembrolizumab with close monitoring after discussion with patient regarding risks and benefits.
At age 73, resumed pembrolizumab 200 mg IV every 3 weeks with close pulmonary monitoring.
At age 73, restaging CT showed stable disease. Ground-glass opacities resolved. Primary mass unchanged at 3.7 x 3.0 cm.
At age 73, patient continues on pembrolizumab maintenance with stable disease. Neuropathy improved to grade 1. Pneumonitis has not recurred. Ongoing surveillance.

scenario_blurb: Stage IIIA-IV non-small cell lung cancer without actionable driver mutations (EGFR wild-type, ALK negative, ROS1 negative). Treated with carboplatin/pemetrexed plus pembrolizumab immunotherapy. Mix of adenocarcinoma and squamous cell carcinoma. Some with high PD-L1 expression receiving pembrolizumab monotherapy.
